topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a slap. Subject line: "Regarding your recent piece, 'The Silicon Scape'." My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. The byline wasn't mine. It belonged to a ghost—a name I vaguely recognized from a niche industry forum, someone whose work I’d admired from afar. A slow burn started in my chest, a heat that quickly morphed into a hard, unyielding resolve. I didn't delete the email. Instead, I forwarded it, attaching a single, carefully worded response that laid out the undeniable truth, sharp and precise. I watched the cursor blink on the 'send' button, my jaw set so tight I could feel the muscle strain in my neck. This wasn't over.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the glow reflecting off my tired eyes. The realization hit me with the force of a physical blow: my analysis, my research, my voice—all packaged and sold under someone else's moniker. A tremor ran through my hands, but I forced them still. I stood up, pacing the cramped apartment, the floorboards protesting beneath my weight. Anger simmered just beneath the surface, a low, vibrating hum that demanded release. I pulled out my laptop, not to panic, but to strategize. I began drafting a counter-argument, meticulously cataloging every piece of evidence linking the published work back to my own unique contribution. When I finally typed the rebuttal, the keys clicked with a furious rhythm. I didn't just want to correct the record; I wanted to dismantle the illusion entirely.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug went cold before I even took a sip. It was the silence after the shock that was the loudest thing in the room. I reread the publication details three times, each repetition a fresh injection of bitter frustration. It was a blatant appropriation, a theft of intellectual property masquerading as collaboration. A slow, dangerous smile touched my lips. I didn't waste time on tears or panicked calls. Instead, I started compiling a dossier. Every draft, every source note, every timestamp. My posture straightened, shoulders back, an almost rigid stillness settling over me. I felt a surge of pure, unadulterated refusal. I was going to make them see exactly who was behind the curtain.
